methods.description: Collection of sediment samples from an archaeological profile and initial processing using water screening and manual sorting to recover macro-archaeobotanical remains.

steps.description: Collect sediment samples directly from the profile exposed at the end of excavations carried out in 2014 and 2016 ( Figure 2).
steps.searchable_summary: Collect sediment samples from archaeological excavation profile.

steps.description: Eleven liters of soil were collected from each layer (A to U) by excavating two columns in the north profile measuring 20 × 5 cm wide.
steps.searchable_summary: Collect specific volume of soil from defined layers and columns in profile.

steps.description: Bulk samples weighing ca. 200 g were also collected for phytolith analysis from the same profile.
steps.searchable_summary: Collect bulk soil samples for phytolith analysis.

steps.description: A total of 231 L of soil were water screened using geological 2 mm and 0.5 mm sieves to recover carbonized wood, seeds, fruits, and tubers.
steps.searchable_summary: Water screen soil samples using specific sieve sizes to recover macro-remains.

steps.description: These samples were the same as those used for aforementioned zooarchaeological analysis and were manually sorted at the Laboratory of Tropical Archaeology (University of São Paulo) to separate out archaeobotanical and other archaeological remains.
steps.searchable_summary: Manually sort sieved samples to isolate archaeobotanical remains.

steps.description: The results presented here are based on carbonized remains ≥2 mm (Figure 3).
steps.searchable_summary: Select carbonized remains greater than or equal to 2mm for analysis.

methods.description: Procedure for extracting phytoliths from soil samples using the wet oxidation method, applied to layers A-J.

steps.description: The method used 100 g of soil...
steps.searchable_summary: Weigh out 100g of soil sample.

steps.description: ...which was deflocculated with sodium hexametaphosphate...
steps.searchable_summary: Deflocculate soil sample using sodium hexametaphosphate.

steps.description: ...and removed of all clays...
steps.searchable_summary: Remove clay fraction from soil suspension.

steps.description: ...before being sieved into "sand" (125-250 um) and "silt" (<53 um) fractions, creating two subsamples.
steps.searchable_summary: Sieve soil suspension into sand (125-250 um) and silt (<53 um) fractions.
inputs: Soil suspension with clays removed (from step 3)

steps.description: Carbonate removal was then carried out using hydrochloric acid (37%).
steps.searchable_summary: Remove carbonates from subsamples using hydrochloric acid.

steps.description: and organic matter was removed using nitric acid (67%) heated at 100 • C.
steps.searchable_summary: Remove organic matter from subsamples using heated nitric acid.

steps.description: Phytoliths were then separated using zinc bromide heavy liquid (specific gravity 2.30 g/cm 3 )
steps.searchable_summary: Separate phytoliths using heavy liquid flotation (zinc bromide).
inputs: Subsamples with organic matter removed (from step 6)

steps.description: and were mounted using Entellan mounting medium [56].
steps.searchable_summary: Mount separated phytoliths onto slides using Entellan.

methods.description: Procedure for extracting phytoliths from soil samples using the ultrasonic bath method, applied to layers K-U. This method modifies the wet oxidation method by substituting nitric acid with hydrogen peroxide and using an ultrasonic bath for dispersion, and uses zinc chloride heavy liquid.

steps.description: This method substitutes the nitric acid for organic matter removal with hydrogen peroxide (30%),
steps.searchable_summary: Use hydrogen peroxide (30%) for organic matter removal.

steps.description: and both organic and clay dispersion are aided with the use of an ultrasonic bath set to 60 • C.
steps.searchable_summary: Aid organic and clay dispersion using an ultrasonic bath at 60°C.

steps.description: Phytoliths were separated with zinc chloride heavy liquid (specific gravity 2.30 g/cm 3 )
steps.searchable_summary: Separate phytoliths using heavy liquid flotation (zinc chloride).
inputs: Processed sample (implied after dispersion and organic removal)

steps.description: and mounted in Permount mounting medium (for combined results, see Figure 4).
steps.searchable_summary: Mount separated phytoliths onto slides using Permount.

methods.description: Analysis of starch grains and phytoliths recovered from ceramic fragments to identify plant remains, assess processing damage, and interpret their origin (processed food vs. background soil).

steps.description: A total of 21 individual starch grains and 1large agglomeration (in MC-7) were recovered from the eight ceramic fragments analyzed (Table 1), the majority (n = 18) of which were isolated from the wet brush samples.
steps.searchable_summary: Recover microfossils (starch grains, phytoliths) from ceramic residues, e.g., using wet brush samples.

steps.description: Over half (n = 12) of all starch grains could not be taxonomically identified, either due to their absence in the published literature and/or because they were subject to processing damage.
steps.searchable_summary: Observe recovered starch grains for taxonomic identification.

steps.description: ...either due to their absence in the published literature...
steps.searchable_summary: Compare observed starch grains to published literature for identification.

steps.description: Indeed, most starch grains display signs of physical damage in the form of cracks (emanating from the hilum and/or margin of the grain), pressure facets...
steps.searchable_summary: Observe starch grains for physical damage features.
outputs: Observations of physical damage on starch grains

steps.description: ...heat damage in the form of hilum openings (caused by water loss), hilum projections (dark-colored projections caused by toasting), flat relief (i.e., a weak extinction cross), and/or gelatinization (partial or total loss of 3D structure, see Cascon...
steps.searchable_summary: Observe starch grains for heat damage features.
outputs: Observations of heat damage on starch grains

steps.description: The accumulation of these damages on the same starch grains or starch grains from the same artefact (e.g., MC-6) indicates that several plant foods were milled or pounded before cooking, and that both toasting and boiling were common.
steps.searchable_summary: Interpret starch grain damage patterns to infer plant food processing methods (milling, pounding, toasting, boiling).
inputs: Observations of physical and heat damage on starch grains (from steps 4, 5)

steps.description: The phytolith assemblages from the ceramic residues (MC 1-8) mirrored almost exactly those reported in the soils, both in terms of their diversity (which includes the presence of grass, arboreal, and palm phytoliths in every sample), and in the percentages in which they occurred.
steps.searchable_summary: Analyze phytolith assemblages recovered from ceramic residues.

steps.description: The phytolith assemblages from the ceramic residues (MC 1-8) mirrored almost exactly those reported in the soils, both in terms of their diversity... and in the percentages in which they occurred.
steps.searchable_summary: Compare ceramic residue phytolith assemblages with soil phytolith assemblages.
inputs: Ceramic residue phytolith assemblage data (from step 7)
Soil phytolith assemblage data (from other analyses, e.g., Methods 2 & 3 results)

steps.description: They are, therefore, interpreted to represent a "background" signal from the surrounding soils, rather than plants that were processed or contained in the ceramic vessels. This same phenomenon has been reported in similar analysis from other sites [7,9].
steps.searchable_summary: Interpret phytolith assemblage comparison results to distinguish background signal from processed plant remains.
outputs: Interpretation of phytolith origin (background vs. processed)

steps.description: The one exception to this was the identification of a hooked trichomephytolith in MC-8 similar in size and morphology to those produced in the pods of common beans (Phaseolus sp., Figure 6P) [94]. This identification remains tentative, however, due to the occurrence of very similar phytoliths in the Panicoideae grass Orthocladalaxa [9,95].
steps.searchable_summary: Attempt tentative identification of specific phytolith morphotypes.
